title: Assisting a stuck lamb at lambing
subtitle: Recognising a malpresentation and correcting it before the ewe is exhausted
excerpt: Most ewes lamb on their own. When they do not, the gap between a ewe straining without progress and a keeper who acts can decide whether the lamb lives. This tutorial covers how long to wait, what a normal presentation feels like, and the three most common malpresentations and their corrections.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Better Returns Programme: Lambing (2013). https://ahdb.org.uk

DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Sheep (1998). https://www.gov.uk

SAC Consulting, Lambing management guidance. https://www.sac.co.uk

content.text: Most ewes lamb on their own. When they do not, time matters: a ewe straining hard without progress for half an hour needs you to check her, and a keeper who waits too long can lose the lamb and tire the ewe out. This tutorial covers the standard wait, what a normal presentation feels like, and the three most common malpresentations with how to correct each one.
For a small keeper, the standard rule is thirty minutes of

with no lamb showing before you go in. A ewe that is restless, pawing the ground, and looking at her flanks may still be in the first stage of labour; leave her alone. A ewe straining hard with nothing at the vulva after half an hour needs investigating.

attrs.body: In a normal presentation, the front hooves come first, soles down, with the head resting on the lower front legs. You should be able to feel the tongue of the lamb just inside the vulva if one or two hooves are visible. The head follows, then the shoulders, which are the widest point.
attrs.title: Normal presentation

content.text: Wash your hands and arms to the elbow with soap and water. Apply lubricant freely. Approach the ewe from behind. Have someone hold her head or work her against a corner. If you are not confident with the anatomy, have your vet's number ready before you start and call early rather than late.
Insert one hand gently and feel what is there. In
you feel both front hooves and the head just behind them. The three most common
are: head-back (both front legs forward but the head turned back to one side), one or both legs folded at the knee, and breech (tail first with the hind legs forward into the ewe's belly).

items.fix: Push the lamb slightly back to make room, cup the head in your palm, and guide it forward until the nose rests on the front legs. Assist with the next contraction once the head is in position.
items.cause: The head has turned sideways in the uterus, usually to the right.
items.symptom: Head turned back: two hooves visible but no head

items.fix: Push back gently, cup the hoof, and bring the leg forward to its full extension before pulling.
items.cause: One or both front legs folded at the knee, so the knee rather than the hoof presents.
items.symptom: One or both knees folded (knee joint presents first instead of hoof)

items.fix: This is the hardest of the three: the hindquarters must pass through the pelvis without legs to push against. Call the vet if you are not experienced with breech deliveries.
items.cause: Lamb is reversing out, hind legs tucked forward into the uterus.
items.symptom: Tail or hindquarters visible with no legs (breech)

content.text: Clear mucus from the lamb's nostrils straight away. Spray the navel with strong iodine within the first hour to prevent joint ill entering through the cord. Check the ewe for a second lamb: twins are common in most lowland breeds. Record the ewe tag, lamb tag, birth weight, and any assistance given.
